summary: Joel describes his typical workday pattern of procrastination followed by intense productivity. He spends hours checking email and browsing the web before finally forcing himself to open his code editor. Once he starts coding, he enters a flow state and works nonstop for hours. The core problem he identifies is the mysterious gap between deciding to work and actually starting — a transition he can't reliably make.
key_points: Procrastination follows a predictable, repeating cycle of deciding to start work then defaulting back to low-effort activities like email and web browsing
The hardest part of productive work isn't the work itself but the activation energy required to begin
Once past the starting barrier, flow state takes over and productivity becomes effortless and time-dissolving
money_quotes: Somewhere between step 8 and step 9 there seems to be a bug, because I can't always make it across that chasm.
launch the damn editor and write code nonstop until I don't realize that it's already 7:30 pm
key_insight: The biggest obstacle to programmer productivity isn't the difficulty of the work but the mysterious inability to reliably cross the chasm between intending to start and actually starting.
